data.title: PennantPark Investment: Q2 Earnings Indicate Continued Weakness
data.description:
Overview

Business Development Companies continue to be one of the most efficient vehicles to capitalize on the higher interest rate environment. However, these unusual times have revealed that not all BDCs are constructed with the same quality. PennantPark Investment (NYSE:PNNT) recently reported their Q2 earnings and I wanted to take this as an opportunity to revisit the fund and assess the performance and outlook. I previously covered PNNT and issued a hold rating due to the lack of NAV growth and fact that net investment income levels didn't fully support the distribution.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that PNNT's price has fallen by more than 8.6%, with most of that decline happening over the month because of the market uncertainty related to President Trump's tariff proposals. When including all distributions paid out over the same time frame, the total return is barely positive at 2.4%. The main appeal of maintaining a position in a BDC like PNNT is the generous starting dividend yield of about 14.7%. As an added bonus, PNNT pays its distributions out on a monthly basis.

Despite the recent price decline, I still don't think that PNNT trades at an attractive valuation yet. Following the recent earnings, I believe there could still be additional downside for shareholders, since the NAV has not grown. Sales and repayments continue to outpace capital allocated towards new investment activity as well. There's a lot that can go wrong from this point forward, so it's crucial that management makes the best strategic moves for PNNT going forward. So let's first take a look at the most recent earnings report and the overall risk profile.

Q2 Earnings & Risk Profile

PNNT recently reported their Q2 earnings and the results were a bit disappointing. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.18 per share, which was a decrease from the prior quarter's net investment income of $0.20 per share. Total investment income was $30.6M ,which was also a reduction from the prior year's amount of $35.9M. At least PNNT was able to slightly reduce expenses down to $19.2M to help offset the lower earnings for the quarter. Net investment income for the quarter declined to $11.4M, from a prior amount of $14.2M. The earnings continue to disappoint and lead to a lower NAV.

  • Q1 2025: $7.56 per share
  • Q2 2025: $7.48 per share

A declining NAV is one of the clearest indications that an underlying strategy isn't effective at generating a sufficient amount of income. It can indicate that management isn't actively growing its base assets enough through new investment activity, or it can hint that the distribution may be too generous. Therefore, I personally require the NAV growth to improve over the remainder of 2025 before my stance on PNNT changes.

In more positive news, PNNT was able to allocate $176.8M towards new investment activity. This capital was allocated towards three new portfolio companies and fifty-two existing portfolio companies with a weighted average yield of 10.7%. Unfortunately, this investment capital was outpaced by sales and repayments totaling $263.1M, ultimately leaving PNNT with a loss of net assets over the quarter. In order for PNNT's fundamental strength to improve, it needs to allocate a lot more capital towards new investments.

I am a bit cautious as I don't feel confident in many BDCs' ability to efficiently allocate new investment capital at the moment. The higher interest rate environment has made it difficult to find attractive deals in the market, so slowing asset growth is a trend we are seeing across the BDC sector right now. I am worried that management may start to loosen their investment criteria, effectively sacrificing quality underwriting just to pursue growth.

I believe that one of the most important aspects to measuring the risk profile of a BDC is to monitor the non-accrual rate. Non-accruals represent the rate of borrowers that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. As a result, these borrowers on non-accrual status aren't actively contributing towards a BDC's earnings and they reduce the overall quality of the portfolio. When I previously covered PNNT, non-accruals amounted to 4.3% of cost and 1.5% of fair value.

The latest reporting indicates that non-accruals have substantially improved. Non-accruals now sit at 1.6% of cost and 0.4% of fair portfolio value. This non-accrual rate is very competitive when putting it against some peer BDCs in the sector. However, non-accruals are only one piece of the pie and a low rate may not necessarily mean that a BDC will outperform. Just as an example, here are the non-accrual rates for peer BDCs and their respective performance on a YTD basis.

  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 0.9% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • Saratoga Investment Corp. (SAR): 0.3% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • SLR Investment (SLRC): 0.6% non-accrual rate at fair value.

Fund Strategy

According to the most recent portfolio breakdown, PNNT has assets totaling $1.2B that is spread across a range of different debt investments. We can see that diversity is a major focus of PNNT's strategy. Financials are estimated to account for the largest slice of assets with a weight of 21.5%. This is followed by varying weights to Business Services, Consumer Products, Healthcare & Pharma, and Transportation just to name a few.

However, I don't really prefer the investment structure that PNNT focuses on. For instance, only 41% of its debt investments are structured on a first lien senior secured basis. First lien debt sits at the top of the capital structure and is my preferred form of debt because it has the highest priority for repayment. This structure can act as a defensive layer for BDCs because it reduces the probability that all invested capital is lost in a bad transaction. If a borrower defaults and needs to liquidate assets, PNNT would be entitled for repayment before other forms of debt. The entire portfolio breakdown by type is as follows:

  • 41% First Lien Secured Debt
  • 29% Preferred and Common Equity
  • 18% Subordinated Debt
  • 10% US Government Securities
  • 2% Second Lien Secured Debt

The large focus on equity investments increases the risk profile since equity sits on the bottom of the corporate capital structure and has the lowest priority for repayment. Therefore, the equity layer is where losses are most typically absorbed. Equity investments may not produce consistent levels of cash flow, so they depend on the valuation growth of underlying borrowers. This structure allows PNNT to also experience capital appreciation during periods where performance is strong.

Lastly, 91% of the portfolio is structured on a variable rate basis. In theory, this should allow PNNT to capitalize from the higher interest rate environment. A higher interest rate directly translates to a greater amount of interest income that can be collected from its borrowers. However, a higher interest rate can also put stress on any portfolio companies that are already underperforming.

Updated Valuation

Despite the price decline in recent months, PNNT still trades at a smaller discount than usual. For instance, PNNT currently trades at a discount to NAV of about 13.8%. Over the last three year period, PNNT was traded at an average discount to NAV of about 18.14%. What stands out to me within the BDC sector, is that high quality BDCs tend to consistently trade at a premium valuation while lower performers stay in the discount territory. At the moment, I think the recent quarterly reports justify the discounted valuation. Therefore, I don't believe this to be an opportunity, but instead as a sign of the underlying weakness.

Wall Street analysts have an average price target of $6.75 per share. This indicates a small potential of about 1.6% from the current level but I don't think this is anything to get excited about. While I do certainly think that PNNT's price could rebound back up to this price target, I just don't see any large catalysts that can drive growth.

I do think that PNNT could be more successful in changing its growth trajectory in a lower interest rate environment. A lower interest rate could possibly lead to lower net investment income levels, since borrowers will see a reduction in their interest expense being paid. Additionally, a lower interest rate environment may stimulate an environment of increased borrowing activity since it becomes more affordable for businesses to hold debt on their balance sheet. An influx of new opportunities could be exactly what PNNT needs to fuel a greater amount of new investment activity.

Unfortunately, I don't think we are likely to see any sizeable interest rate cuts happen anytime soon. The Fed decided to leave rates unchanged in their most recent meeting as they observe economic data. Markets are still trying to determine what the ripple effects of President Trump's tariff policies will be, inflationary data remains higher than anticipated, unemployment continues to trend upward, and consumer spending habits are shifting. Even if the Fed slightly reduces rates, I don't think it will be enough to drive the needle higher for PNNT, since rates still remain near decade-highs.

Questionable Dividend

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.08 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 14.7%. If we multiply this monthly payout by three, we get a quarterly payout of $0.24 per share. As mentioned, net investment income for the quarter amounted to $0.18 per share. This means that the distribution is not currently being fully supported by the actual earnings of PNNT. I personally think this will eventually lead to a distribution cut, as it makes the most sense. A cut would allow the NAV to grow and create healthier fundamentals for PNNT.

However, management seems confident that they'll eventually be able to support the distribution with net investment income. PNNT has been leaning heavily on its undistributed spillover income to make the shortcoming in earnings. PNNT still has spillover income to support two years worth of distributions but I question whether it's actually worth it to maintain exposure to a BDC that needs to rely on spillover income. This could possibly be a case of management kicking the can down the road and delaying the inevitable cut, but time will eventually tell.

We are pleased that our secured loan portfolio, with among the lowest portfolio company leverage and most meaningful covenants in the industry, is positioned defensively and continues to perform well. Additionally, our dividend stream is supported by substantial spillover income as we look to rotate equity investments over time. - Art Penn, CEO

Management may be waiting for interest rates to get reduced in the hopes of this presenting additional opportunities for growth. I would like to see improvements here eventually so that net investment income can exceed the distributions but given the current circumstances, I am not initiating a position yet.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my hold rating on PNNT as the latest Q2 earnings left a lot to be desired. Net investment income levels slightly decreased, as well as the NAV per share. Sales and repayments outpaced new investment activity, also causing a decline in net assets. The earnings still don't fully support the distribution and this is one of my main concerns. Management believes that they'll eventually get earnings back above the distributions but I remain cautious. I don't typically prefer to entrust a BDC that is reliant on spillover income to support consistent dividend income within my portfolio. While the recent price drop could certainly be an opportunity for patient investors to accumulate, I prefer to wait on the sidelines for now.

data.title: SQQQ: Don't Go Short This Market
data.description:
Introduction

In November, I initiated my coverage of the UltraPro Short QQQ ETF (NASDAQ:SQQQ) by urging investors not to go short the stock market, despite the fundamental and technical reasons to. I discussed how much fear there was in the air, as well as exuberance, and why it didn't make a lot of sense to go short and speculate that we were about to crash.

Very few people ever actually nail the timing, and even fewer are more than just lucky. It's very human to carry a fairly self-centered world view, which the kids call "main character syndrome." It's easy for us to assume that we are right, for us to ignore faults in our logic, missing information, and other factors that others can see more plainly. That makes it even easier to catastrophize news stories and believe that a crash in imminent. While we may be right at some point, as shown in the example below, hindsight is 20/20.

SQQQ is a 3x short Nasdaq 100 fund, meaning it gains three points for every point NDX/QQQ loses. It is a tool that investors (read as: traders) can use to be short without needing to employ their own margin and sell short the index. However, it is best not to be short the market currently, for several reasons.

The primary reason is shown in the performance of SQQQ since that article was published, which is now down about 20%.

Note that being short was profitable at one point, but only for a brief moment, and only if investors were able to correctly time the markets, which is very difficult. In early April, when stocks fell precipitously on 'Liberation Day,' the fund jumped to an over 50% gain, but quickly retraced all of it.

Many investors who did catch the run-up likely lost some on the way down, and others likely piled in toward the top, hoping to catch the momentum. Instead, they were served a reversal.

Risks of Leveraged Funds

Of course, there are folks who will ignore my warnings about SQQQ and use it anyway, so I want to make sure that I offer up some warnings, because leveraged funds like SQQQ carry their own risks stemming from their construction and daily-reset behavior.

There are three warnings that I think investors should consider when looking at any daily-resetting leveraged fund:

1) The SEC notes:

Leveraged and inverse ETFs typically are designed to achieve their stated performance objectives on a daily basis. Some investors might invest in these ETFs with the expectation that the ETFs may meet their stated daily performance objectives over the long term as well. Investors should be aware that the performance of these ETFs over a period longer than one day can differ significantly from their stated daily performance objectives and may potentially expose investors to significant and sudden losses.

2) The fund's prospectus notes:

The Fund uses leverage and will lose more money when the value of the Index rises than a similar fund that does not use leverage. The use of leverage increases the risk of a total loss of your investment. If the Index approaches a 33% gain at any point in the day, you could lose your entire investment. As a result, an investment in the Fund may not be suitable for all investors. The use of leverage increases the volatility of your returns. The cost of obtaining this leverage will lower your returns.

3) This review of MSTX, the "Case Study For Bad Leveraged ETFs" as a cautionary tale.

The Market Can Go Up for Any Reason

It's not that I'm very bullish right now and believe you shouldn't short because the market will go up, but because there are too many reasons for markets to go up for any one person to keep track of. Combine that with the aforementioned "main character syndrome" most investors tend to operate under (some more than others), and you have a recipe for disaster. It's easy to think we have all the facts already, and have seen something the market hasn't.

Right now, it feels as though the market is using every excuse it can get to go up, especially following the large drop from early April. The market continues to grind up despite issues like a negative GDP reading in Q1, the Fed holding rates at 4.25-4.5% indefinitely while they "wait and see," and heightened fear among businesses across the board about the economy.

Dip Buyers, Unite!

Instead of going short, I have been taking time this year to pare back my equity holdings, instead opting to be "less long," as I've been putting it. The reason is fairly straightforward: I am happy making less money if I lose less money.

This kind of risk management is difficult to espouse, because in bull markets, all folks want to talk about is past performance. On my articles discussing the potential for losses ahead of us like "Things Aren't Looking Good," and "Don't Get Caught in the Bull Trap," which were meant to be tales of caution for investors, I received a ton of comments along the lines of:

Markets will go up because they always go up.

This kind of irrational exuberance is exactly what gets folks burned, when they haven't considered their risk tolerance. They believe that the markets come with some kind of imaginary protective put led by "dip buyers."

These dip buyers do exist, and there are plenty of them, don't get me wrong. They are primarily smaller investors, evidenced by the massive volume during the sell-off, followed by a rally all the way back up with lowered volume. There are fewer hands moving the market higher than were bringing it lower, and these are the dip buyers.

Despite this, the market could be bought up by these folks moving forward as well, and the bottom of their ability to "buy the dip" has yet to be fully tested by markets.

Despite the volume continuing lower in the chart above, the price continues higher. News stories that drive the market narrative don't need to be great news, just not catastrophic, it seems.

Forget the Rambling Bears

This culture among investors could drive markets higher and higher, regardless of valuations, like the historically high Shiller PE ratio for the market, often touted as one of the more reliable indicators of future returns. Of course, we bounced off the old top before the sell-off in early April, but are rapidly approaching it again as stocks recovered in a shorter timeframe than earnings have been able to catch up.

I can go on all day about all the reasons the market has to fall, but it doesn't matter, because there are more reasons for it to go up. For example, despite tariff chaos being the primary reason for the last steep drawdown, that sent SQQQ up 50% in just a few days, the market has fully recovered already, and SQQQ is now down below where it started pre-tariff announcement. Holding from before the announcement through now has been unprofitable, despite having been up incredibly at some point.

It's easy to be glib and say, "They were idiots for not selling out when they had the chance," but very difficult to actually do it at the moment. Most investors just sat and watched the whole thing.

Tariff news? We made a deal with the UK, which, in one commenter's words, is like "taking your cousin to prom." While I was bullish on the news of the deal, it is fairly small peanuts compared to the gargantuan task of ironing out a fair and competitive deal with China. Despite little progress being made toward that deal, and the only real announcement we have to work with being that the embargo-level tariffs have been lowered (for now), but will resume in 90 days. We've kicked the can down the road, but markets have already recovered, like a deal was reached, and a crisis was avoided.

'Liberation Day' saw the US threaten tariffs of 34% on all Chinese goods, and markets plummeted.

The deal reached in Geneva to alleviate trade war damage that followed the Liberation Day announcement was to set the rate at 30% only temporary, a mere 4% lower than the initial rate. Yet, the market moved on, and moved up.

Being short was not the right move following the initial drop, and I continue to think it isn't, no matter how much writing seems to be on the wall.

Besides that, the announcement of the tariffs that caused the crash-out in the first place was done intentionally after market close, so one had to be short beforehand, holding through the event and into the next day. That is a level of risk I do not think is prudent for most folks, as it was just pure speculation since nobody knew (and most were surprised) about what the rates were actually going to be.

Conclusion

No matter what red flags I see in the economic data, fundamental market data, or technical patterns in charts, the market can find a reason to rise. There are few reasons for the market to fall, and they usually come by surprise when they happen, so investors going short are effectively guessing and speculating. This isn't suitable for most people, and investors should avoid funds like the UltraPro Short QQQ ETF for this reason.

Being short is not something I would recommend for anybody right now, at least not across the full market. Of course, there is some benefit to being tactically long and short certain factors or investment themes, but in the past, I have argued that investors are better off making these into pair trades that mitigate some market risk. Shorting on its own, especially if one intends to hold as a hedge, is likely to end in unreasonable losses due to timing concerns and the inability for news-savvy investors to predict actual market trends.

Instead, to reduce risk, I have been advising folks to consider raising their cash allocations while all the recent market turmoil has been ongoing. If we do have another crash, and we may, having less in the market will be a boon. Pulling all the way out is also irresponsible, as there may be more gains to be had regardless of the headlines and rambling bears.

Stay safe out there, and if you intend to finish this article and go short anyway, remember this line:

Position sizing is often more important than security selection.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: M2 Charts Look Good
data.description:
This blog is one of the few places you will find information about the all-important M2 measure of the US money supply. I've been covering this since I first began this blog in the summer of 2008.

The reasons that few follow M2 are several: 1) the Fed apparently pays no attention to the money supply, 2) not many Wall Street analysts pay attention to M2, and 3) making sense of money supply is difficult in the absence of any concrete measures of money demand. I try to fill in those gaps.

These links will take you to a more in-depth discussion of this topic, but to simplify: Inflation happens when the supply of money exceeds the demand for it. M2 is arguably the best measure of money supply. Currency is arguably a proxy for money demand, as is the ratio of M2 to nominal GDP.

The reason surging money supply in 2020 through early 2022 didn't create inflation is that money demand also surged. The reason that declining money supply over the past two years has not been deflationary is that money demand also declined. Today, money supply and money demand appear to be in balance, which explains why inflation has been relatively low and stable.

Chart #1

Chart #1 shows the level of the M2 money supply. M2 grew at about a 6% pace from 1995 through 2019, a period characterized by relatively low and stable inflation, which further implies that money supply and demand were in balance.

M2 then exploded from March 2020 through early 2022, but inflation started rising in early 2021; this implies that money demand started to collapse in early 2021. For the past year or so, M2 growth has picked up, but inflation has slowed, which implies that money demand has begun to stabilize at a lower level (see Chart #2).

Chart #2

Chart #3

Chart #3 shows currency in circulation (a lot of which is held overseas, by the way). I have argued that currency is a proxy for money demand because people only hold currency if they want to; unwanted currency can simply be deposited in a bank, whereupon it disappears from circulation and is returned to the Fed.

Like M2, currency increased at about a 6% annual rate from 1995 through 2019. It then surged over the next year or so, even though we saw relatively low and stable inflation. The growth of currency then began to slow (and inflation to pick up) in early 2021, and has now returned to its long-term growth path, coinciding with relatively low and stable inflation.

In sum, money supply appears to be growing at a moderate but sub-normal rate, while money demand appears to be somewhat soft. That's not a recipe for rising inflation, and instead signals, in my view, that inflation will remain low and relatively stable for the foreseeable future.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Starwood Property Q1: Expected To Grow In 2025
data.description:
I rate Starwood Property Trust, Inc. (NYSE:STWD) a Strong Buy, for income-focused investors interested in real estate investment trusts (REITs). The REIT is a hybrid in that it focuses on mortgages and lending but also includes some real properties, creating some protection from typical interest rate risks. The most important factors that influence my opinion of the REIT are:

  • Currently pays a quarterly dividend yielding 9.6% annually.
  • Has paid a $0.48 quarterly dividend for the past 11 years.
  • is a Hybrid REIT that invests in mortgages and owns real properties.
  • Managed by Starwood Capital Group CEO & Chairman Barry Sternlicht
  • Reputation for risk management, with strong process of checking credit and buying high quality properties.
  • Experienced management team with an average of 29 years of industry experience.

Full disclosure, I own shares of STWD for the purpose of income. It’s the largest holding in my portfolio. Over the years, I’ve listened to Barry Sternlicht on the earnings calls to understand the business. In every case, he has proven the company to be in a leadership position, anticipating both threats and opportunities, and turning defaults into profitable sales. Barry often appears on TV as a guest on CNBC or Bloomberg as a respected authority on commercial real estate. His comments are well respected due to his history of success in the field.

Seeking Alpha Quant does not cover the REIT.

The Gold Standard of Hybrid REITs

Based in Miami Beach, Florida, Starwood Property Trust, Inc. operates as a real estate investment trust in the United States and internationally. The company operates through four segments: Commercial and Residential Lending; Infrastructure Lending; Property; and Investing and Servicing.

Since the IPO in 2009, the total capital deployed is over $103 billion, with a current portfolio of $25.3 billion over multiple business segments. The company is focused on providing a secure quarterly dividend.

The chart below maps out all of the businesses that have been added to the original commercial lending REIT which went public in 2009. STWD now does mortgage back securities, loan servicing, loan origination, residential and infrastructure lending, and in 2014 it added real properties. In my view, the actual ownership of buildings combined with a steady dividend helps to contain the volatility of the market price.

Some of the properties acquired come from loan defaults. If the note holder doesn’t pay, STWD can sue for foreclosure and take the property as real estate owned. The company is very effective at taking possession of a property that is worth more than the loan, then in essence repairing and flipping it back to the market while making a profit doing it.

Of the approximately $25.3 billion in total assets, over half 54% are commercial loans and 13% are owned properties. U.S. office loans account for about 10% of the total assets.

Starwood Capital Group

Founded in 1991 by Barry Sternlicht, Starwood Capital Group has approximately $115 billion in AUM. The group invests in just about every major real estate asset class possible including private and public real estate investment trusts, equity, debt, and energy. Real property management includes residential (multifamily), hotels, data centers, retail, and office/life science buildings.

Q1 2025 Earnings Drove Share Price Higher

On May 9th, the company declared Q1 earnings. The financial highlights were as follows:

  • Earnings of $0.33 and distributable earnings of $0.45 per share
  • Invested $2.3 billion, including $1.4 billion in commercial lending and record $0.7 billion in infrastructure lending.
  • Closed $1.3 Billion After Quarter End, Including $0.9 Billion in Commercial Lending
  • Issued $500 Million of Corporate Debt, Extending Average Maturity to 3.7 Years
  • Current Liquidity of $1.5 Billion
  • Paid quarterly dividend of $0.48 per share for over a decade.

Overall, this was a positive quarter for STWD, and the stock price rose in appreciation. One of the key metrics to track is the distributable earnings (DE). In this case, DE of $0.45 was below the quarterly dividend of $0.48 but the company assured investors that the earnings shortfall would be made up in future months, therefore there was no concern about a dividend cut. In fact, Jeffrey DiModica, President, said there is:

$1.5 billion of unrealized property gains which we can harvest to create incremental distributable earnings.

In my opinion, keeping the dividend at $0.48 per share fixed is very attractive to investors because it becomes predictable and serves to hold the stock's value from violent price swings.

Starwood Outperforms Both Equity and Mortgage REITs

An investment of $10,000 in STWD over 10 years, with dividends reinvested, would double over the time period to $21,350. This performance is superior to the overall REIT market represented by Vanguard Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ), and the mortgage REIT market, as represented by VanEck Mortgage REIT Income ETF (MORT).

Valuation

Since 2014, STWD has paid $0.48 dividend each quarter, which is comforting to investors due to its predictability.

In a comparison to peers in the mortgage arena, STWD stands out for having the lowest debt to equity ratio at 257.4% This is partly due to the fact that they own actual physical buildings within the portfolio, which makes it less risky than all mortgage debt.

Annaly Capital Management, Inc. (NLY) is the largest of the peers, as ranked by market cap in the chart above. It stands out for having the highest debt to equity ratio of 679%, but one of the lower 5-year total returns of 65.1%.

AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) has the highest dividend yield of 15.9%, with the second highest debt to equity ratio at 656.9%. It also has the lowest 5-year total returns of 43.9%.

Rithm Capital Corp. (RITM) has the lowest dividend yield of 8.7% and the highest 5-year total returns of 226.6%.

Blackstone Mortgage Trust, Inc. (BXMT) has one of the lower dividend yields of 9.5% and one of the lower 5-year total returns of just 51.4%. This has been one of my favorite mortgage REITs, or mREITs, beyond STWD. However they cut their dividend last year and the share price fell. In following the mREIT, I believe there will be upside this year, especially as the Fed may be incentivized to cut rates later on in the year. Also, BXMT is expanding beyond mortgages to diversify the portfolio, which could reduce risk. I’m bullish on BXMT.

Arbor Realty Trust, Inc. (ABR) is the only mREIT with a 5-year dividend growth rate that is positive, generating 8%. Its 5-year total return of 193.5% is second highest, making it one of the favorite mREITs around. One word of caution – every once in a while, a short seller will drop a press release creating a negative story about the company which makes its price drop quickly in one day. Usually the allegations are puffery, so it doesn’t go anywhere, creating panic for those who own ABR, and an opportunity to buy shares if you realize what is going on.

Apollo Commercial Real Estate Finance, Inc (ARI) with a dividend yield of 10.1% this mREIT is another of my favorites. It has strong management, good risk controls, lower debt to equity ratio of 366.3%, and an admirable 5-year total return of 160.5%.

Outlook

What I like about STWD is that the company is ever-changing and growing. The team has a strong history in my view of anticipating challenges ahead or seeing opportunities to exploit. Very often investors hear about new projects on the earnings call, which can translate into a bounce in stock price. As part of Starwood Capital Group, STWD has access to a group of seasoned professionals that are constantly challenging the status quo. The March 2025 Investor deck had a great quote:

Future growth opportunities will come from a combination of leveraging STWD’s existing platform and pursuing new investments with meaningful synergies with Starwood Capital Group’s core competencies.

Scott Bessent, the Secretary of the Treasury, said the administration is pursuing a policy that has three components, like a three-legged stool. They include taxes, trade, and deregulation. Assuming the tax policy stays intact, that will be good for real estate since there are currently tax advantages for developing and owning real estate.

STWD Goes ex-dividend on approximately June 27, 2025.

Risks To Rating

The largest risk to STWD is something happening with the key man, Barry Sternlicht, in my opinion. He represents the company well and is a strong presence in the business media talking up the properties. Should he become ill or something, that could adversely impact the share price.

The second largest risk is macroeconomic forces such as interest rates, employment, and inflation. Given the current economy, we are expecting potential rate cuts at the end of the year and the impact of tariffs may be lower than first thought.

Bottom Line

I rate Starwood Property Trust a Strong Buy for income-focused investors interested in real estate investment trusts. STWD is a hybrid REIT meaning it has physical assets in addition to mortgages. The leadership of Barry Sternlicht and the Starwood Property Group is a core competency behind STWD, adding star value as well as real estate expertise.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the REIT are the current dividend paying 9.6% annually, the history of consistent payout of $0.48 quarterly dividends for the past 11 years, and the expertise of the management team that brings an average of 29 years per executive. Finally, over the past 10 years, STWD has doubled in total return, outperforming the equity and mortgage REIT sectors overall. For these reasons, STWD is my largest holding, and I recommend it as a Strong Buy.

data.title: Celsius: Watch International Growth Before It's The Narrative
data.description:
Celsius Holdings, Inc. (NASDAQ:CELH) is eyeing an attractive international growth runway through fresh market launches, gaining increasing momentum while staying under the market’s radar. Other narratives, including Celsius’ distribution-related hiccup and competitive pressure, remain the market’s focus point after a disappointing Q1. Regardless, the energy drink company has bright growth prospects ahead both within the US and internationally.

I maintained a Buy rating in my previous February article on the stock, “Celsius: Weighing The Alani Nu Acquisition.” The stock has since returned 21%, outpacing the S&P 500’s (SP500) slight -2% decline.

Watch Celsius’ International Growth Before It’s the Narrative

While the market’s eyes have largely been on Celsius’ shelf performance in the U.S. and on distribution timings that have caused severe sales turbulence, I believe that Celsius’ international growth is a narrative to watch closely. Additional markets are likely to become an increasingly important growth driver for the Celsius brand, and international growth could well become the future growth narrative past the recent Alani Nu acquisition.

After striking the distribution deal with PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP) in August 2022, Celsius’ international operations have been on a clear upward trajectory; Celsius’ increased scale has enabled the company to also focus on additional markets beyond the U.S. From late 2022 forward, international sales have gained significant momentum as the brand’s global recognition has improved through marketing. Continuing the trend, international revenues reached $22.8 million in Q1, up 41% year-on-year.

Instead of PepsiCo, Suntory Beverage & Food Limited (OTCPK:STBFY) is responsible for most of Celsius’ international distribution in newer markets, but Suntory still enables great international scaling despite not being as large a conglomerate as PepsiCo.

The international market potential is clear. Celsius’ international revenues have so far been dependent on small markets, primarily Sweden, Finland, and Norway, but the company has recently entered massively larger markets. Celsius announced an entry into Canada, the UK, and Ireland for Q2 ’24, an entry into Australia and New Zealand with a Q4 ’24 launch, and an entry into France with a broad launch only in 2025. Celsius also more recently announced a 2025 launch in Belgium and Luxembourg. Based on great international revenue momentum in early 2025 already, the additional international markets are clearly setting up for success - Celsius' international presence will be past just the Nordics in the future. Celsius is also eyeing additional market launches for a significantly long and meaningful growth runway.

Granted, although growth has been rapid, international revenues still accounted for just 7% of the total topline result – scaling international sales to meaningful velocity may still take a while.

The recently launched markets still have minimal single-digit market shares, enabling a significant growth runway. Monster Beverage Corporation (MNST) generated 40% of its 2024 revenues outside of the U.S., and a similar share for Celsius would imply approximately 57% total revenue growth potential from 2024 for the company. Secular growth in energy drink consumption adds potential on top, expected to grow at a 7.9% 2024-2030 CAGR globally.

Celsius Q1 Review: A Disappointment, But the Market Is Forward-Looking

Celsius had quite disappointing Q1 sales. Revenues totaled $329.3 million, down -7.4% year-on-year and missing Wall Street’s already quite cautious expectations by $13.0 million. The company related the weakness to four primary factors in the quarter’s earnings call: slowed velocity, timing and structure of the U.S. distribution incentive program, increased retail promotional programs, and a challenging Q1 ’24 comparison period due to Celsius Essentials' launch. As per the second point, the PepsiCo distribution hiccup still seems to be causing some revenue turbulence for now.

Slowed velocity and increased promotional programs clearly reflect a more challenging competitive landscape – Celsius’ retail performance reflects a sharp decline in market share year-on-year for the brand, declining from 12.3% to 10.9%. Competitive pressure from, especially Red Bull has continued to cause concern over Celsius’ shelf performance. At the same time, Celsius’ retail-level market share performance did stabilize sequentially, and the brand will soon face easier comps in market share.

Earnings performed nearly as expected as the adjusted EPS of $0.18 missed Wall Street’s consensus by just $0.01. The gross margin expanded by 110 basis points to 52.3%, reflecting a strong performance despite increased promotional programs. Adjusted EBITDA still declined by -20.8% to $69.7 million based on increased operating costs, reflecting increased investments in global sales, marketing, and the organizational structure. While revenues didn’t scale in line with operating costs for Q1, I don’t believe that the earnings decline is cause for concern. Celsius is scaling its expenses ahead of more meaningful revenue growth internationally and is preparing for increased U.S. velocity. Upcoming revenue growth should soon offset the upfront expense increases.

Celsius is setting up for a better shelf performance, being very encouraging after a slow start to 2025. The company entered Q2 with much better retail momentum, and with shelf space gains, the Playa Vibe, Retro Vibe, and Mango Lemonade launches, and plans for a significant marketing campaign for the summer season, I believe that the Celsius brand should stabilize its market share and even potentially gain back some recent losses.

Further, the Alani Nu acquisition closed in early April, setting up a much better outlook. The acquired brand has continued to grab market share at an impressive pace, even enabling Celsius to increase 81 basis points of U.S. market share on a pro forma basis in Q1. Alani Nu’s market share stood at 5.3% in Q1 already.

Combining the improved growth prospects, after a disappointing Q1, I believe that Celsius is set to achieve meaningful organic growth. Growth should also trickle down to earnings through stronger gross margins and improved scale - the market is forward-looking, and there should be good organic growth ahead.

CELH Remains Attractively Priced

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Celsius. I now estimate adjusted EBITDA profitability instead of GAAP EBIT.

As the distribution-related revenue hiccup should subside and Celsius’ financials include Alani Nu, Celsius is still set to report massive 2025 growth. I now estimate revenue growth at 60% for the year. Afterward, 2026 growth will still benefit inorganically from Alani Nu, and I expect 20% growth for the year. The Alani Nu acquisition, international growth, and general consumption growth contribute to a good organic growth outlook, and I now estimate a 10.0% CAGR in the 2026-2034 period with 3% perpetual growth afterward.

I expect Celsius’ adjusted EBITDA margin to ultimately reach 24% through growth and the Big Beverages manufacturing acquisition, still well below Monster’s already achieved margin level. The cash flow conversion from EBITDA should be relatively good, enabled by minimal capital expenditures but worsened by occasional adjusted cash expenses.

The estimates put Celsius’ fair value at $58.45, 54% above the stock price at the time of writing. The stock bottomed in early 2025, and given the impressive growth prospects, Celsius remains an attractive investment with significant upside.

The fair value estimate is up from $47.86 previously due to a lower WACC.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 7.18%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 6.46% interest rate, 200 basis points above the risk-free rate. I again expect a modest 10% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.46% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimates for the U.S. Seeking Alpha now estimates Celsius’ beta at 0.66; the previous 1.01 estimate was conservative given energy drinks’ defensive nature. With a liquidity premium of 0.1%, the cost of equity stands at 7.42% and the WACC at 7.18%.

Takeaway

Celsius’ momentum has continued internationally, and 2024 market launches should have a significant growth runway ahead. The scale of international revenues is small for now, but can become the main growth narrative in the future as market launches gain shelf space. The market’s narrative is more focused on Celsius’ financial results, where a distribution-related hiccup and competitive pressure caused pressure in Q1.

Looking forward, Celsius’ growth outlook in the U.S. is still good, furthered by the Alani Nu acquisition. Celsius’ stock has significant upside, and as such, I remain with a Buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
